summary: Research + design an auto-create-course feature (scorecard scan / OCR / fallback data) for courses GolfCourseAPI lacks

design.headlineApproach: SCAN THE CARD: scorecard-photo capture -> server-side vision extraction (Claude Sonnet 4.6 behind a Cloud Function proxy, cloned from the existing searchCourses skeleton) -> structured 18-hole JSON pre-fills the EXISTING community-scorecard editor (showScorecardEditor, src/pages/courses.js:730) -> member confirms grid side-by-side with their photo -> dual-write to courses/{id}: holes/allTees (the shape live scoring actually consumes, playnow.js:697-704) PLUS communityData{status:'community_added', source:'scorecard-scan'} (the existing trust layer). The course is born UNVERIFIED with the photo as its evidence artifact, and graduates to VERIFIED through the existing 2-peer-verification + ParCoin flow (courses.js:821-841). This wins the category outright: none of the five leading competitors (18Birdies, TheGrint, Golf GameBook, Hello Birdie, Shot Scope) lets a golfer log a round at an unknown course in the moment — every one is email-and-pray or a 48-72h SLA. Parbaughs does it in the parking lot in under a minute. Critically, the paid extraction is an ENHANCEMENT layered on a $0 core: a 60-second chart-it-yourself quick-charter (par-per-hole keypad) ships first with optional photo-attach, so the feature works day one with zero paid dependency and the scan upgrades it later.
design.uxFlow: Saturday, 11:42am, gravel lot at some nine-and-dine GolfCourseAPI has never heard of. Member taps Play Now, types the course name, search comes back empty. Instead of today's bare prompt('State') stub, a sheet slides up: "New ground. This course isn't on the books yet — chart it." Three paths on the sheet, fastest first. (1) CHART IT: name, town, 9/18 toggle, then a quick par keypad — thumb 4-4-3-5 down the card, or tap "just give me total par" if the group is already on the first tee. Sixty seconds, round is live. (2) SCAN THE CARD (Phase 3): point the camera at the scorecard in the cart. Eight seconds later the Caddy lays the extracted grid beside the photo — every par, yardage, and stroke index, with anything illegible left blank, never guessed. Member thumbs down the column, fixes the one smudged yardage, taps Confirm. (3) ATTACH FOR LATER: snap the photo now, chart the bare minimum, let the scan backfill tonight. Either way the round starts immediately against the new course doc. The Caddy announces it in the feed: "First ascent — Tommy charted Range End for the books. Pars on file; the committee will see about the rating." Course detail shows the amber UNVERIFIED badge with "Charted by Tommy" attribution and the original scorecard photo pinned as evidence. First contribution pays the existing +50 ParCoins. Next weekend two buddies who played it tap Verify (+10 each, existing flow) and the badge flips green VERIFIED — at which point the course counts for handicap-grade math. A wrong par doesn't survive the group chat for an hour; that ridicule loop IS the review process for a 20-friend league, and it's the one thing no competitor can copy with a support inbox.
design.dataIntegrityFraming: The "GolfCourseAPI only / zero guessing" rule is about provenance, not vendor exclusivity — and a photographed scorecard is STRONGER provenance than a third-party aggregator, because the printed card is the course's own published source of truth. This is evidence-based entry, not guessing, and here is exactly how it stays honest: (1) EVIDENCE ARTIFACT — the original photo is stored in Firebase Storage and linked from the course doc (scorecardPhotoUrl), so every value traces to a viewable source forever (P9 literal compliance). (2) NULL, NEVER GUESS — the extraction prompt instructs Claude to leave illegible cells null with per-field confidence, never inferring; structured outputs (json_schema) guarantee schema-valid shape. (3) DETERMINISTIC SANITY GATES server-side before anything reaches the client: exactly 18 (or 9) holes, par in {3,4,5}, total par 66-74, stroke index a permutation of 1-18, yardages 80-700, per-tee totals self-consistent. (4) MANDATORY HUMAN CONFIRM — nothing writes to Firestore until the member approves the rendered grid side-by-side with their photo; the confirm screen is the existing showScorecardEditor, the same Founder-verification pattern P9 already uses. (5) PROVENANCE STAMPED — source:'scorecard-scan' (vs existing 'golfcourseapi'), contributorId/contributorName/contributedAt in communityData, exactly mirroring today's pattern at courses.js:791-802. (6) EXISTING TRUST LIFECYCLE UNCHANGED — api_only -> community_added (UNVERIFIED badge, courses-detail.js:42-66) -> verified at 2 peer verifications (courses.js:829). Scan-created courses wear the badge until peers verify. (7) DEFAULTS NEVER MASQUERADE — when a fallback source lacks rating/slope, render "estimated (72.0/113)" explicitly instead of letting the silent courses.js defaults pose as sourced data (P10: no silent fallback). One honesty prerequisite: today's contribution loop is itself silently broken — submitAddCourse (courses.js:511-523) never persists to Firestore, and submitScorecardData writes communityData.holePars which live scoring NEVER reads (playnow.js:697-704 consumes only allTees[].holes/c.holes). Fixing those two dead-ends is the first commit; any extraction feature built on top would inherit both bugs.
design.buildPlan: STEP 0 — INTEGRITY FIXES (prerequisite, $0, no gates): (a) submitAddCourse (src/pages/courses.js:511-523) calls PB.addCourse (in-memory only, src/core/data.js:266-304) and never writes Firestore — add the syncCourse call (src/core/sync.js:220) so manually added courses stop evaporating. (b) submitScorecardData (courses.js:773-819) updates only {communityData, par} (verified at :805) — make it ALSO write c.holes + a single-tee allTees entry so contributed pars reach the in-round hole grid, since playnow.js:697-704 resolves pars exclusively from allTees[].holes then c.holes. (c) Fix the editor pre-fill bug: courses.js:734 feeds {par,...} objects from c.holes into integer comparisons at :754-756, silently selecting par 3 for API-sourced courses. STEP 1 — EDITOR EXTENSION: grow showScorecardEditor (courses.js:730-771) into the universal confirm surface: yardage + stroke-index rows, multi-tee tabs, 9/18 toggle (drop the hardcoded 0..17 loop at :779). It becomes the single confirm screen for manual entry, scan results, and fallback-API imports. STEP 2 — QUICK-CHARTER SHEET: replace the three prompt('State') stubs (courses.js:298 quickAddCourseFromDir, playnow.js:643 quickAddCourse, rounds.js:865 quickAddCourseForRound) with one shared sheet: name/town/9-18/par-keypad (total-par shortcut) + optional photo-attach (reuse compressPhoto, courses.js:638; store in Firebase Storage). Dedupe guard: slug + fuzzy-name check against existing courses before create. Write {holes, allTees(single tee), par, communityData{status:'community_added', source:'member-charted'}, quickAdd:true, addedBy}. NO firestore.rules change needed — courses are already member-writable (firestore.rules:543-546); Storage rules need one scoped path for scorecard photos. STEP 3 — FALLBACK SOURCES: on GolfCourseAPI miss in courseSearchWithApi (courses.js:313-352) and _searchGcApiForDirectory (:207-228), try OpenGolfAPI (api.opengolfapi.org, no key, 1,000/day vs primary's 50/day, live-verified to return per-tee rating/slope + per-hole par/handicap/yardage for York PA home courses): fetch /search then /tees + /holes (search payload has null ratings — must hit /tees), map onto _extractTeeData's shape (courses.js:258-274), stamp source:'opengolfapi', add ODbL attribution line, vendor the 2.2MB dump as an offline hedge against the young project dying. OSM Overpass for international misses: par only, no slope/rating — persist source:'osm' and render rating/slope as "estimated". Link ncrdb.usga.org from the editor as the human lookup for authoritative rating/slope (manual only — it 403s automation and scraping is a legal RED). STEP 4 — EXTRACTION FUNCTION (the paid layer): new Gen1 Node 22 us-central1 HTTPS function extractScorecard cloned from the searchCourses skeleton (functions/index.js:144-175): same origin allowlist + per-IP rate limit (functions/lib/rate-limit.js; suggest 10 scans/hour/IP) + zod body schema (functions/lib/validators.js pattern). Accepts base64 photo (client downsamples to <=1568px long edge, ~1,568 image tokens), calls Claude Messages API with structured outputs json_schema: {tees:[{name,color,rating?,slope?,par,yards,holes:[18x{par:3|4|5, yardage, handicap}]}], illegible cells null, per-field confidence}. Sonnet 4.6 default (~$0.03/scan), auto-retry low-confidence scans on Opus (~$0.06). Server runs the sanity gates, returns the grid; client pre-fills the Step-1 editor; on confirm, the existing importAndSelectCourse write shape (courses.js:414-458) lands holes/allTees/communityData in one update. Anthropic key lives in Functions secret config, never the PWA bundle (P8). GATES TO CLEAR: AMD-018 gate 1 (function deploy — same path task #24 is already exercising) via task-queue/founder/; AgentShield scan after wiring; Legal & Compliance pass on ODbL attribution wording; no ParCoin economy code changes (reuse existing +50/+10), so gate 4 stays untouched.
fallbackChain: 1. GolfCourseAPI (existing primary, worldwide, already wired via the searchCourses Cloud Function proxy; source:'golfcourseapi')

2. OpenGolfAPI automatic fallback (free, no key, 1,000 req/day, live-verified per-tee rating/slope + per-hole par/handicap/yardage for the league's York PA courses; source:'opengolfapi'; ODbL attribution; vendored 2.2MB dump as offline hedge)
3. OSM Overpass for international/remaining misses (par-per-hole only — no slope/rating; source:'osm'; rating/slope rendered explicitly as 'estimated (72.0/113)', never as sourced truth)

4. Scorecard photo scan (Claude vision via extractScorecard Cloud Function -> sanity gates -> member confirms grid beside photo in the extended editor; source:'scorecard-scan'; photo stored as the evidence artifact; UNVERIFIED until 2 peer verifications)

5. Minimal manual quick-charter (name + town + 9/18 + par-per-hole keypad, or total-par + tee shortcut; optional photo-attach for later scan backfill; NCRDB link for authoritative rating/slope lookup; source:'member-charted'; UNVERIFIED badge + Caddy first-ascent line)

founderDecisions.decision: Extraction engine for the scan feature (the only spend in the design)
founderDecisions.options: (a) Claude vision via Cloud Function — Sonnet 4.6 default ~$0.03/scan, Opus retry ~$0.06, Haiku option ~$0.01; one-step photo-to-structured-JSON, schema-guaranteed, best accuracy on table-from-photo per 2025-26 benchmarks; requires a new Anthropic API key + billing account. (b) Google Cloud Vision OCR — $0 at league volume but returns flat text + coordinates, requiring a brittle hand-built grid parser that breaks per card layout (L effort, MEDIUM integrity risk from right-digit-wrong-row errors). (c) Tesseract.js — $0 OSS on-device, but 70-80% phone-photo accuracy with table structure discarded; the confirm screen becomes the member retyping half the card, defeating the feature.
founderDecisions.recommendedDefault: Claude vision, Sonnet 4.6 default with Opus auto-retry on low confidence. At league scale even all-Opus is ~$6 per 100 scans/year — engineering effort goes into verification instead of layout parsing. P4 paid-LAST is honored because Phases 0-2 ship the full feature at $0 first; the scan is a paid enhancement, not a dependency.

founderDecisions.decision: AMD-018 gate 1 pre-authorization: deploy the extractScorecard Cloud Function (and Anthropic key into Functions secret config)
founderDecisions.options: Approve alongside the task #24 deleteMyAccount deploy already on this path, or hold Phase 3 until a later deploy window. No firestore.rules change is needed either way (courses are member-writable, firestore.rules:543-546).
founderDecisions.recommendedDefault: Approve via task-queue/founder/ bundled with the task #24 deploy window. Authority gate, not spend — Blaze invocation cost is negligible at 20 members.

founderDecisions.decision: Handicap gating: do UNVERIFIED (member-charted or scan-created) courses count toward handicap-grade math before reaching VERIFIED?
founderDecisions.options: (a) Strict: rounds log immediately and show to-par, but the course feeds handicap/wager-relevant math only after VERIFIED (2 peer verifications) or Commissioner promotion. (b) Loose: count immediately with the UNVERIFIED badge as the only guard.
founderDecisions.recommendedDefault: Strict (a). Wrong par silently corrupts handicap and ParCoin wager math downstream — the round memory is never blocked, only the math waits for the existing trust flow. Keeps the zero-guessing posture defensible.

founderDecisions.options: (a) Reuse the existing amounts unchanged: +50 first contribution, +10 per verification (already live at courses.js:811-813, :838) — zero economy-code change, AMD-018 gate 4 untouched. (b) New bonus tier for full scans (pars + yardages + SI), which touches ParCoin earn rules and requires gate-4 pre-auth.
founderDecisions.recommendedDefault: Reuse existing +50/+10 unchanged (a). The first-ascent Caddy moment is the real reward; revisit a scan bonus post-launch if contribution velocity disappoints.

founderDecisions.decision: Adopt OpenGolfAPI as the automatic free fallback (with ODbL share-alike attribution)
founderDecisions.options: (a) Adopt with 'Data © OpenGolfAPI (ODbL)' attribution in the courses UI, wording routed past the Legal & Compliance reviewer, plus the vendored data dump as a longevity hedge (project is ~2 months old, 1-2 GitHub stars). (b) Skip it and lean entirely on scan + manual for API misses.
founderDecisions.recommendedDefault: Adopt (a). Live-verified to cover the league's actual home courses with exactly the fields the app consumes including per-tee rating/slope (which OSM lacks entirely), at 20x the primary's free-tier quota. The vendored dump neutralizes the young-project risk.

phasedPlan.ships: Three bug fixes everything else sits on: (1) submitAddCourse Firestore persist (courses.js:511-523 currently loses manual courses on refresh), (2) submitScorecardData also writes holes/allTees so community pars reach live scoring (today playnow.js:697-704 never reads communityData.holePars — the contribution loop dead-ends), (3) editor pre-fill object-vs-int bug (courses.js:734 vs :754-756). Plus E2E coverage seeding the real doc shape per the scrambleTeams lesson.

Shared 'Chart this course' sheet replacing the three prompt() stubs (courses.js:298, playnow.js:643, rounds.js:865): name/town/9-18/par-keypad + total-par shortcut + optional photo-attach (compressPhoto -> Storage, stored as evidence for later scan backfill). Extended showScorecardEditor (yardage/SI rows, multi-tee tabs, 9/18). Dedupe guard, source:'member-charted', UNVERIFIED badge with contributor attribution, Caddy first-ascent feed line, existing +50/+10 ParCoin flow, NCRDB manual-lookup link. Round logs immediately — beats all five competitors' request-and-wait the day it ships.

OpenGolfAPI automatic fallback on GolfCourseAPI miss (search -> /tees -> /holes mapped onto _extractTeeData shape, source:'opengolfapi', ODbL attribution after Legal pass, vendored dump hedge). OSM Overpass par-only fallback for international (source:'osm', rating/slope rendered 'estimated'). Provenance labels on the course detail page so no default masquerades as sourced data (P9/P10).

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 3 — Scan the card (paid enhancement, gated)
phasedPlan.ships: extractScorecard Cloud Function (searchCourses skeleton clone: origin allowlist + rate limit + zod) calling Claude Sonnet 4.6 with structured-output json_schema, Opus retry tier, null-never-guess prompting, server-side sanity gates (18 holes, par in {3,4,5}, total 66-74, SI permutation, yardage bounds, per-tee total consistency). Pre-fills the Phase-1 editor for confirm-before-save; Phase-1 attached photos become retroactively scannable ('backfill scan'). Anthropic key server-side only. Requires Founder decisions 1+2 (spend + AMD-018 gate 1) and an AgentShield pass after wiring.

Google Cloud Vision TEXT_DETECTION (within the 1,000/month free tier) as an independent cross-check engine — flag any cell where the two engines disagree for member attention on the confirm screen. Contribution/verification analytics on the course directory. Commissioner promote-to-verified control.

design.mvpThatShipsNow: Phases 0+1, start today with zero paid dependency, zero new secrets, zero Cloud Function deploys, zero firestore.rules changes (courses are already member-writable per firestore.rules:543-546): fix the three silent-loss bugs (submitAddCourse never persists; community pars never reach live scoring because playnow.js consumes only allTees/holes; editor pre-fill bug), then ship the 60-second 'Chart this course' quick-charter at all three course-miss touchpoints — name, town, 9/18 toggle, par-per-hole keypad with a total-par shortcut, optional scorecard-photo attach stored in Firebase Storage as the evidence artifact for later extraction. Course doc lands as source:'member-charted' with the UNVERIFIED badge, contributor credit, the Caddy's first-ascent line, and the existing +50/+10 ParCoin verify flow. The round logs in the parking lot before the group text does — an instant, honest category win over every competitor's email-and-wait, and the photo-attach means every Phase-1 course is already queued for the Phase-3 auto-extract upgrade the moment the Founder approves the spend.

findings.area: Parbaughs course search/add/scorecard integration map (code research)
findings.summary:
HOW COURSE DATA WORKS TODAY.

(1) DATA SHAPE. Courses live in Firestore `courses/{id}` (Firestore-authoritative, loaded into memory via syncCoursesFromFirestore src/core/sync.js:122-133 -> PB.setCoursesFromFirestore src/core/data.js:318-321; any active member may write the whole doc per firestore.rules:543-546). Course doc shape, built in PB.addCourse (src/core/data.js:266-304): {id (slugified name, 20 chars), name, loc, region, rating (float, default 72), slope (int, default 113), par (int, default 72), photo, reviews[], tee (default-tee name), yards, holes[], allTees[], lat, lng, source:'golfcourseapi', quickAdd, addedBy, phone, website, communityData}. Per-tee shape in allTees[] (built by _extractTeeData, src/pages/courses.js:258-274): {name, rating, slope, par, yards, holes:[18 x {par, yardage, handicap}]}. c.holes is the flattened holes array of one preferred tee (White > Silver > tees[1] > tees[0], courses.js:260). communityData shape (written by submitScorecardData, courses.js:791-802): {status:'api_only'|'community_added'|'verified', holePars:[18 ints], parTotal, teeName, slope, rating, contributorId, contributorName, contributedAt, verifications:[{uid,name,at}]}. Rounds carry their OWN per-hole copies independent of the course doc: holeScores[], holePars[], holeYards[], holesPlayed, holesMode, tee, yards, plus stat arrays (fir/gir/putts/bunker/sand/upDown/miss/penalty) and a scorecardPhoto field (src/core/data.js:344-394, photo at :362).

(2) ADDING A COURSE THE API LACKS — yes, create-new paths exist TODAY, but ALL are metadata-only stubs with zero hole data: (a) quickAddCourse (src/pages/playnow.js:643-667), quickAddCourseForRound (src/pages/rounds.js:865-878), quickAddCourseFromDir (src/pages/courses.js:298-307) — each fires a bare prompt() for a 2-letter state, then writes {rating:72.0, slope:113, par:72, quickAdd:true} locally + to Firestore. No holes, no tees. (b) Full Add-Course form renderAddCourseForm/submitAddCourse (courses.js:496-523) — name/loc/state/rating/slope/par fields only, no hole entry, and BUG: submitAddCourse never writes Firestore (PB.addCourse is in-memory; data.js:302 'No save()'); the course persists only if a later review (courses.js:687) or helpful-vote (courses.js:718) happens to call syncCourse (sync.js:220). (c) Hole pars arrive only AFTER the fact via the community scorecard editor: showScorecardEditor (courses.js:730-771) renders 18 par dropdowns (3/4/5 only) + ONE tee name/slope/rating; submitScorecardData (courses.js:773-819) Firestore-updates {communityData, par:parTotal} and awards +50 ParCoins on first contribution (:811-813); verifyCourseData (courses.js:821-841) flips status to 'verified' at 2 verifications (+10 coins). Editor entry is gated on having played the course (courses-detail.js:38, 56-65). No yardage, no handicap index, single tee, 18-hole hardcoded (loop 0..17 at courses.js:779).

(3) GOLFCOURSEAPI INTEGRATION. Search is client -> Cloud Function proxy `searchCourses` (functions/index.js:144-175): origin allowlist + 60/min IP rate limit (functions/lib/rate-limit.js) + zod query schema (functions/lib/validators.js:70-73) -> GET https://api.golfcourseapi.com/v1/search. The shared API key sits in localStorage 'golfcourse_api_key', seeded from Firestore config/api_keys.golfCourseApi at startup (src/core/router.js:633-637; saved by saveGolfApiKey courses.js:473-488). Two client search surfaces: directory search (filterCourseDirectory courses.js:168-205 -> _searchGcApiForDirectory:207-228 -> importDirApiCourse:276-296) and the shared typeahead courseSearchWithApi (courses.js:313-352) used by playnow.js:587, rounds.js:859, syncround.js:57, wagers.js:510, bounties.js:150 -> importAndSelectCourse (courses.js:414-458). Import maps API tees via _extractTeeData and dual-writes the full course incl. holes/allTees (courses.js:292-293, 435-436).

(4) AUTO-EXTRACTION PLUG-IN POINTS. (i) The three prompt()-based quick-add stubs and submitAddCourse — exactly where a new course is born without data. (ii) showScorecardEditor as the human-confirm screen for extracted values (it already pre-fills from cd.holePars || c.holes at courses.js:734 — note pre-fill bug: c.holes elements are {par,...} objects while the editor compares ints at :754-756, so API-sourced pre-fill silently selects par 3). (iii) A new Cloud Function cloned from the searchCourses skeleton (CORS+rate-limit+zod) for server-side extraction — AMD-018 gate 1 applies to deploy. (iv) Firestore write path needs NO rules change (courses allow write: amIActive(), firestore.rules:545). CRITICAL SHAPE GAP: live scoring resolves hole pars ONLY from allTees[].holes then c.holes (playnow.js:697-704); communityData.holePars is NEVER consumed there, and submitScorecardData updates only the par total — community-contributed pars never reach the in-round hole grid. Any auto-extraction must write holes/allTees (the consumed shape) in addition to communityData (the trust/status shape).

(5) WHAT communityData ALREADY SUPPORTS: 3-state trust lifecycle (api_only -> community_added -> verified at 2 peer verifications, courses.js:829), contributor attribution, verification ledger, ParCoin incentives (+50/+10), status badges + gated CTA buttons on the detail page (courses-detail.js:42-66). NOT supported: yardages, stroke-index/handicap, multiple tees, 9-hole courses, photo evidence, pending-review queue.

options.name: Client-side extraction at the quick-add touchpoints
options.how: Replace the prompt('State') stubs (courses.js:298, playnow.js:643, rounds.js:865) and submitAddCourse (courses.js:511) with a capture step (photo/URL) -> extraction -> pre-filled showScorecardEditor confirm -> dual-write {holes, allTees, communityData{status:'community_added'}, par} to courses/{id}. Rules already permit the write (firestore.rules:543-546).
options.pros: Touches the exact moments a data-less course is created; reuses the existing editor as the confirm UI; no Cloud Function deploy (avoids AMD-018 gate 1); no rules change.
options.cons: Extraction model/API key would be exposed client-side unless proxied; the editor needs extension (yardage row, multi-tee, 9-hole) and has a pre-fill int-vs-object bug (courses.js:734 vs :754) to fix first.
options.cost: Vision-API usage per scan (pennies/scan) if LLM-based; $0 if pure manual fallback
options.dataIntegrityRisk: Extracted pars/yardages enter as communityData status 'community_added' (UNVERIFIED badge) until 2 peer verifications — existing trust flow covers it; P9 requires the confirm screen so no value lands unseen.

options.name: Server-side extractScorecard Cloud Function (searchCourses-pattern clone)
options.how: New Gen1 HTTP function copying the searchCourses skeleton (functions/index.js:144-175): origin allowlist + per-IP rate limit (functions/lib/rate-limit.js) + zod schema (functions/lib/validators.js) -> accepts scorecard image -> vision model -> returns {tees[{name,rating,slope,par,yards,holes[{par,yardage,handicap}]}]} matching _extractTeeData output (courses.js:263-272) so the client import path (importAndSelectCourse courses.js:414-458) is reused verbatim.
options.pros: Key stays server-side; output shape identical to the GolfCourseAPI import path so holes/allTees flow into Play Now (playnow.js:697-704) with zero new client plumbing; rate-limitable.
options.cons: Cloud Functions deploy is an AMD-018 gate-1 Founder pre-auth item; adds a billable function; image upload size handling needed (rounds already compress via compressPhoto, courses.js:638).
options.cost: Firebase invocations (negligible at 20 members) + vision-API usage per scan
options.dataIntegrityRisk: Same communityData trust gate applies; server can also stamp source:'extracted' alongside contributorId for provenance.

options.name: No-AI baseline: complete the manual create-course path (prerequisite either way)
options.how: Extend showScorecardEditor (courses.js:730-771) into a full create flow: yardage + handicap rows, multi-tee, 9-hole support; wire it into renderAddCourseForm; fix submitAddCourse's missing Firestore persist (courses.js:511-523 has no db write / syncCourse call); fix the pre-fill bug (courses.js:734 feeds {par,..} objects into int comparisons at :754-756); make submitScorecardData also write c.holes so contributed pars reach live scoring (today playnow.js:702-703 never reads communityData.holePars).
options.pros: Zero cost; fixes two real bugs (lost manual courses, dead-end community pars) that ANY extraction feature would sit on top of; smallest surface.
options.cons: Still manual typing for 18 holes x tees — the friction the feature is meant to remove; no wow factor.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: Low — purely closes existing silent-loss paths; improves P9 traceability.

findings.recommendation: Do option 3 first regardless of extraction approach — submitAddCourse's missing Firestore write and the communityData.holePars-never-consumed gap mean today's contribution loop silently dead-ends; an auto-extraction feature built on top would inherit both. Then plug extraction in via option 2 (server-side function cloned from the searchCourses proxy) because its output can exactly mimic the GolfCourseAPI tee shape (_extractTeeData, courses.js:258-274), letting the existing import path populate holes/allTees (what Play Now actually consumes, playnow.js:697-704) AND communityData (the trust/verification layer) with no new client data model. Use showScorecardEditor as the mandatory human-confirm screen (P9) and keep the existing UNVERIFIED -> verified ParCoin flow as the trust mechanism. Note AMD-018 gate 1 (function deploy) needs Founder pre-auth; Firestore rules need no change (courses are already member-writable, firestore.rules:543-546).
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Zach\smoky-mountain-open\src\pages\courses.js:730-819 (editor + submit), :258-307 (tee shape + quick-add), :414-458 (import path); src\core\data.js:266-304 (course shape), :344-394 (round hole shape); src\pages\courses-detail.js:34-66 (communityData status flow); src\pages\playnow.js:697-704 (hole-par consumer); functions\index.js:144-175 (searchCourses proxy); firestore.rules:543-546 (courses write rule)

findings.summary: All three options were costed and accuracy-checked against published evidence. (a) Claude vision is the only option that returns the 18-hole table as structured data in one step: send the photo (downsampled to ~1568px long edge = ~1,568 image tokens via the official width*height/750 formula) plus a strict JSON schema via structured outputs (output_config.format json_schema, supported on Haiku 4.5/Sonnet 4.6/Opus 4.8), and the API guarantees schema-valid JSON: holes[1..18] with par, stroke_index, and yardages keyed by tee name. Cost ~ $0.01/scan (Haiku 4.5), ~$0.03 (Sonnet 4.6), ~$0.06 (Opus 4.8) — negligible for a 20-member league scanning a handful of courses per year. 2025-26 benchmarks consistently show vision LLMs beating traditional OCR on table-from-photo extraction (GPT-4o 90.5% vs Textract 82% on line items; Claude Opus cited as leader on complex structured extraction). (b) Google Cloud Vision TEXT_DETECTION is effectively free at this volume (first 1,000 units/month free, then $1.50/1,000) but returns a flat text stream + bounding boxes — reconstructing an 18xN grid from coordinates across wildly varying scorecard layouts (decorative fonts, colored tee rows, front/back split tables) is a large, brittle custom-parsing project; Document AI Form Parser (the GCP product that does do tables) runs ~$30/1,000 pages = $0.03/page, i.e., same price as Sonnet with weaker semantics. (c) Tesseract.js is free and in-browser, but the Tesseract maintainers themselves confirm it loses table structure entirely ('flat stream of cell contents'), and documented accuracy on phone photos drops to 70-80% — on a colored, stylized scorecard it would silently misread digits (385 -> 365 yardage) with no semantic layer to catch it. The only serious OSS prior art (joshshep/golfr, OpenCV+Keras) illustrates how heavy the hand-rolled CV route is even for one fixed layout. Data-integrity framing: the printed scorecard IS the course's primary source, so photo extraction is evidence-based and compatible with the 'zero guessing / GolfCourseAPI only' rule — provided extraction is verified, not trusted. Required verification layer regardless of engine (P9-aligned): (1) store the original photo in Storage and link it to the course doc so every value traces to source; (2) deterministic sanity checks before write — exactly 18 holes, par in {3,4,5}, total par 66-74, stroke_index is a permutation of 1-18, yardages 80-700 and per-tee totals self-consistent; (3) member-facing confirm screen rendering the extracted grid beside the photo (Founder-verification pattern) before Firestore commit; (4) prompt Claude to mark illegible cells null + per-field confidence rather than guess — explicitly instruct 'never infer a value you cannot read'. Architecture constraint: the Anthropic API key cannot ship in the PWA bundle (security-paramount, P8) — the call must proxy through a Cloud Function, which touches the AMD-018 gate #1 deploy path (same path as the in-flight deleteMyAccount deploy, task #24).

options.name: Claude vision API (Haiku 4.5 / Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.8 retry) behind a Cloud Function
options.how: Member photographs the scorecard; PWA uploads to a Cloud Function endpoint; function downsamples to <=1568px long edge (caps image at ~1,568 tokens) and calls the Messages API with the image (base64) + a strict json_schema via output_config.format: {holes:[{hole:1-18, par:enum[3,4,5], stroke_index:1-18, yardages:{tee_name:int}}], tees:[{name,color,rating?,slope?}]}. Prompt instructs: transcribe only what is legible; null + flag anything unreadable; never infer. Server runs sanity checks (18 holes, par sum 66-74, SI permutation of 1-18, yardage bounds), then returns the grid to the PWA for member confirm-against-photo before Firestore write. Retry hard photos at a higher tier model.
options.pros: One step photo->structured JSON, no layout parsing code; schema-valid output guaranteed by the API; benchmarks show vision LLMs lead traditional OCR on table-from-photo (GPT-4o 90.5% vs Textract 82%; Claude cited as leader on complex structured extraction); reads scorecards as semantic tables (knows par vs yardage vs SI rows), so it self-corrects column drift OCR cannot; can self-report per-cell confidence; handles handwriting, skew, decorative fonts far better than OCR.
options.cons: Requires an Anthropic API key + billing account (new external dependency); key must live server-side -> Cloud Function proxy -> AMD-018 gate #1 (Founder pre-auth for function deploy); LLMs can occasionally hallucinate a digit on blurry cells, so the confirm-screen + sanity checks are mandatory, not optional; per-scan latency ~5-15s.
options.cost: ~$0.01/scan Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5 per MTok), ~$0.03/scan Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15), ~$0.06/scan Opus 4.8 ($5/$25); image ~1,568 tokens + ~500 prompt in, ~1,500-2,000 JSON out. 100 scans/year on Sonnet = ~$3. Batch API halves it if ever needed.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: LOW-MEDIUM raw, LOW with guardrails: highest raw transcription fidelity of the three, but failure mode is a confidently-wrong digit, so P9 requires the photo-beside-grid member confirmation, null-don't-guess prompting, and deterministic sanity checks before any Firestore write. Photo stored as the traceable source artifact.

options.name: Google Cloud Vision OCR (TEXT_DETECTION / DOCUMENT_TEXT_DETECTION) + custom grid parser
options.how: Cloud Function (project already on GCP/Blaze, so zero new vendor setup) sends the photo to the Vision API; response is full text + word/symbol bounding boxes; custom code clusters boxes into rows/columns by y/x coordinates, identifies the par/yardage/SI rows by label matching, and rebuilds the 18-hole grid. Same sanity-check + member-confirm layer on top.
options.pros: Effectively free at league volume (first 1,000 units/month free, then $1.50/1,000); same GCP project and IAM as Firebase — no new vendor, no new key custody story; deterministic engine (no hallucination — it returns what it saw or nothing); strong raw character accuracy on printed text; could also serve later as a cheap cross-check engine against Claude output.
options.cons: Returns a flat text stream + coordinates, NOT a table: rebuilding 18xN from geometry across every course's unique layout (split front/back tables, colored tee rows, rotated cards, decorative fonts) is a large, brittle parsing project that breaks on each new design — exactly the hand-rolled-CV burden the golfr project illustrates; GCP's actual table product (Document AI Form Parser) costs ~$30/1,000 pages = $0.03/page, matching Sonnet's price with weaker semantics; misassigned cells (right digits, wrong tee row) are the dominant error mode and are hard to detect automatically.
options.cost: $0 at league volume (under 1,000 units/month free tier); $1.50/1,000 scans beyond; Document AI Form Parser variant ~$0.03/page. Real cost is engineering time on the layout parser.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: MEDIUM: characters are read faithfully but structure is reconstructed by heuristics — a yardage landing in the wrong tee row passes a per-value eyeball check while corrupting the course record. Needs the same confirm screen plus stronger structural validation.

options.name: Tesseract.js on-device OCR (OSS, in-browser) + custom grid parser
options.how: PWA loads tesseract.js (WASM) and runs OCR client-side on the photo — no server, no API, no per-scan cost; same custom row/column reconstruction problem as option (b) but with a weaker engine; results feed the same sanity-check + confirm screen.
options.pros: Fully OSS and $0 forever (P4-perfect on paper); runs offline in the browser — no key, no Cloud Function, no AMD-018 gate; photo never leaves the device (privacy win); battle-tested library with an active community.
options.cons: Evidence is decisively against it for this input class: Tesseract maintainers confirm tables come back as an unstructured flat stream ('for real tabular data extraction you need a different tool entirely'); documented accuracy on phone photos drops to 70-80% (vs 95-99% on clean 300-DPI scans); scorecards are near-worst-case input — colored row backgrounds (tee colors), decorative typefaces, logos, gridlines, small digits; WASM payload (~2-4MB+ traineddata) hits PWA load budget; silent digit misreads with zero semantic awareness means the member confirm screen becomes the actual extraction step (member retypes half the card), defeating the feature.
options.cost: $0 — OSS, on-device. Hidden cost: engineering the layout parser plus an expected high manual-correction rate per scan.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: HIGH: 70-80% accuracy on phone photos means ~1-2 wrong cells per 18-hole row set, silently. Wrong par/SI corrupts handicap and wager math downstream (ParCoin economy touches AMD-018 gate #4). Fails P9's 'every visible value traces to source' in practice even though the source exists, because transcription fidelity is the weak link.

findings.recommendation: Claude vision behind a Cloud Function proxy, with Sonnet 4.6 as the default extraction model and Opus 4.8 as the automatic retry tier for low-confidence/illegible scans (Haiku 4.5 is viable at ~$0.01/scan if cost ever mattered — at league scale it does not: even all-Opus usage is ~$6/100 scans/year). It is the only option where the engineering effort goes into verification instead of layout parsing: structured outputs guarantee schema-valid 18-hole JSON in one call, while options (b) and (c) both require building and forever-maintaining a brittle bounding-box-to-grid parser that breaks on every new course's card design — and (c) additionally starts from 70-80% phone-photo accuracy with table structure discarded. The data-integrity story is strongest here too, and it must ship as part of the feature, not after: (1) original photo stored in Firebase Storage and linked from the course doc (the traceable source); (2) prompt-level 'null, never guess' rule with per-field confidence; (3) deterministic post-extraction validation (18 holes, par in {3,4,5}, total 66-74, stroke index = permutation of 1-18, yardage 80-700, per-tee totals self-consistent); (4) member confirms the rendered grid side-by-side with their photo before anything writes to Firestore. This makes scan-created courses evidence-based per the zero-guessing rule — the scorecard is the course's own published source, stronger provenance than a third-party API for courses GolfCourseAPI lacks. Two gates to surface in planning: the Anthropic API key is a new secret (gitignored local + Functions config, never in the PWA bundle, per P8/security-paramount) and the proxy lands on the AMD-018 Cloud Functions deploy path already being exercised by task #24. Optional later hardening, free: run Google Cloud Vision TEXT_DETECTION (within the 1,000/month free tier) as an independent cross-check engine and flag any cell where the two engines disagree.
findings.evidence: Claude image-token formula + per-image cost tables: https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/vision.md (width*height/750; 1568-token cap at <=1568px long edge; Sonnet ~$4.70/1K images at 1.19MP). Model pricing (Haiku $1/$5, Sonnet $3/$15, Opus $5/$25 per MTok): claude-api skill model table (cached 2026-05-26). GCV pricing: https://cloud.google.com/vision/pricing ($1.50/1,000 after 1,000 free/month). Tesseract table-structure loss: https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/issues/4085; phone-photo accuracy 70-80%: https://medium.com/intelligent-document-insights/tesseract-ocr-265dc2f88992 + https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/ImproveQuality.html. LLM-vs-OCR benchmarks (GPT-4o 90.5% vs Textract 82%; Claude leads complex structured extraction): https://www.vellum.ai/blog/document-data-extraction-llms-vs-ocrs + https://www.koncile.ai/en/ressources/claude-gpt-or-gemini-which-is-the-best-llm-for-invoice-extraction + https://parsli.co/blog/llm-ocr-vs-traditional-ocr. OSS prior art (hand-rolled CV pipeline scope): https://github.com/joshshep/golfr.

findings.area: Fallback golf-course data sources when GolfCourseAPI lacks a course
findings.summary: The app consumes {rating, slope, par, tee, yards, holes, allTees, lat, lng} from GolfCourseAPI (src/pages/courses.js:292; free tier is 50 req/day, ~30,000 courses worldwide per golfcourseapi.com). Four fallback families were researched with live verification. (1) OpenGolfAPI (opengolfapi.org, github.com/opengolfapi) is the standout: free, no API key, 1,000 calls/day/IP anonymous, ODbL dataset (16,908 US courses, 90% hole-by-hole par+handicap coverage) with an MIT REST API on Cloudflare Workers. Live-tested 2026-06-09: search found Heritage Hills Golf Resort in York PA (the league's home turf); /tees returned course_rating 72 / slope_rating 124 / yardage per tee set; /holes returned per-hole par, handicap_index, and per-tee yardages — a near drop-in match for the app's field shape. US-only today; tiny community project (1-2 GitHub stars) so availability is a risk, but the full dataset is downloadable (2.2 MB compressed GeoJSON/CSV/NDJSON with SHA256 checksums), enabling self-hosting. (2) OpenStreetMap via Overpass: taginfo shows 254,861 golf=hole ways globally, 211,736 carrying par (~83%) — roughly 11,700 eighteen-hole-equivalents worldwide including international; a live Overpass bbox query around York PA returned 206 par-tagged holes (~11 courses). Free/ODbL, but NO course rating or slope, and yardage (dist:red/white/blue tags) is sporadic. (3) USGA: the National Course Rating Database (ncrdb.usga.org) is the authoritative US rating/slope source but is a web form only — it 403'd automated fetch — and the GHIN API requires formal USGA approval via the Golfer Product Access program (not public, not free, not feasible for an invite-only app). (4) Official course websites publish scorecards inconsistently (HTML widgets, PDFs, images); industry consensus is scraping them is unreliable — licensed aggregators (iGolf, golfapi.io at 42k courses, Golfbert, Zyla/RapidAPI) exist precisely because of this, and all are paid. Course websites are best used as the human-verified source for manual entry / scorecard-photo scan (existing task #26), not as an automated fetch target.
findings.evidence: src/pages/courses.js:292 (consumed field shape); live curl 2026-06-09: https://api.opengolfapi.org/v1/courses/9bb5f2c0-0367-48cd-a493-59273c84915e/tees (slope_rating 124, course_rating 72); https://taginfo.openstreetmap.org/api/4/key/stats?key=par (211,736 ways); https://ncrdb.usga.org/ (403 on automated fetch); https://golfcourseapi.com/ (free tier 50 req/day)

options.name: OpenGolfAPI (api.opengolfapi.org)
options.how: On GolfCourseAPI miss, call GET /v1/courses/search?q=&state=, then /v1/courses/:id/tees and /v1/courses/:id/holes. Map tee_name/course_rating/slope_rating/total_yardage onto the existing allTees shape and hole par/handicap_index/yardages onto holes; store with source: 'opengolfapi' (mirrors the existing source: 'golfcourseapi' pattern at src/pages/courses.js:292). No key needed; add 'Data © OpenGolfAPI (ODbL)' attribution in the courses UI. Optionally vendor the 2.2 MB GeoJSON dump from github.com/opengolfapi/data as an offline safety net.
options.pros: Free, no API key, 1,000 calls/day/IP (vs GolfCourseAPI's 50/day free); live-verified to return exactly the fields the app needs including per-tee rating/slope, which no other free source has; 16,908 US courses with 90% hole-par coverage; already covers the league's actual York PA courses; full dataset downloadable so the dependency can be self-hosted if the service dies; MCP server exists for tooling.
options.cons: US-only today (international expansion is 'help wanted'); very young community project (repos created 2026-04, 1-2 stars) so longevity is unproven; ratings/slope null on some records (search record showed ratings:null while /tees had them — must fetch /tees, not rely on the search payload); ODbL share-alike technically applies to a derived course database — needs a Legal & Compliance reviewer pass on attribution wording.
options.cost: $0. Anonymous tier 1,000 req/day; free authenticated tiers up to 10k/day.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: MEDIUM-LOW. Scorecards sourced from golfpass + community edits, not USGA-official; P9 requires labeling any null-backed defaults (the app already falls back to rating 72 / slope 113 at courses.js:292 — those must render as 'estimated', never as sourced truth). Community-editable data means a bad edit could propagate; pin to the dump or sanity-check par totals (par_total 67-74 for 18 holes).

options.name: OpenStreetMap via Overpass API
options.how: On miss, Overpass bbox query around the course lat/lng (from Nominatim geocode or user pin): way[golf=hole][par](bbox); out tags; — aggregate ref/par/handicap/dist:* tags into a scorecard. Use overpass-api.de or a mirror with the app's existing fetch layer; cache results in Firestore so each course is queried once ever.
options.pros: Truly global (only researched option with international coverage); 254,861 holes mapped, 83% with par; free, no key; ODbL like OpenGolfAPI; per-hole geometry could later power GPS/yardage features; verified 206 par-tagged holes within the York PA bbox.
options.cons: NO course rating or slope anywhere in OSM — handicap math degrades to slope-113 defaults; yardage tags (dist:red etc.) are sporadic; many courses are mapped only as a polygon with zero holes, so hit rate is partial and unpredictable; Overpass public instances have fair-use rate limits and occasional 429/504s; matching a named course to OSM geometry requires geocoding plus fuzzy matching (more code than a REST lookup).
options.cost: $0. Public Overpass instances; self-hostable if volume ever matters (it won't at 20 members).
options.dataIntegrityRisk: MEDIUM. Crowd-sourced with no editorial review; par is usually right but unverified; missing slope/rating means computed handicaps from this source are estimates and must be labeled as such (P9). Stable IDs can change when ways are re-mapped — store a snapshot, not a live reference.

options.name: USGA NCRDB / GHIN
options.how: Two sub-paths: (a) NCRDB (ncrdb.usga.org) as a MANUAL lookup linked from the add-course UI — Founder/member copies authoritative rating+slope into the manual-entry form; (b) formal GHIN API access via the USGA Golfer Product Access program for programmatic official data.
options.pros: The ONLY authoritative source for US course rating and slope — exactly the two fields every free source lacks; NCRDB lookup is free for humans; linking it from the manual-entry form costs nearly nothing and maximizes data truthfulness for the values that drive handicap math.
options.cons: No public API; site returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch, and scraping it would violate ToS — a hard no under the app's legal posture; GHIN API requires USGA application/approval with a qualifying use case, realistic only for licensed handicap products, with unknown timeline and likely cost; US-only.
options.cost: $0 for the manual-lookup link. GHIN/GPA program: application-gated, pricing undisclosed, almost certainly out of scope for a zero-budget invite-only app.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: LOW for data quality (it IS the source of truth) but HIGH for process: manual transcription errors are possible, and any automated scraping would be a legal RED. Use as human-verified input only.

options.name: Official course-website scorecards (scrape or scan)
options.how: NOT as an automated fetch chain. Instead: when all APIs miss, the add-course flow shows the course's website link (OpenGolfAPI records carry website 84% of the time) and offers the planned scorecard-photo scan (existing task #26) so a member photographs the physical/online scorecard and confirms parsed values before save.
options.pros: Course websites are the freshest source after a renovation/re-rating; works for literally any course on earth including the international long tail; the scan-and-confirm flow keeps a human in the loop, which satisfies P9 better than silent scraping; zero licensing cost for member-entered facts.
options.cons: Research confirms scorecards are published as inconsistent HTML widgets, PDFs, and images with no schema — automated scraping is unreliable, breaks constantly, and is why paid aggregators (iGolf, golfapi.io, Golfbert) exist; per-site scrapers are unmaintainable for a 20-member app; some sites prohibit scraping in ToS.
options.cost: $0 for manual/scan flow. (Building a general scraper would burn engineering time indefinitely — effectively the most expensive option.)
options.dataIntegrityRisk: HIGH if automated (OCR/parse errors silently become scorecard truth — direct P9 violation); LOW with the confirm-before-save scan flow since a member visually verifies every value against the official card.

findings.recommendation: Adopt a four-step fallback chain: (1) GolfCourseAPI — keep as primary (existing integration, worldwide, already wired at src/pages/courses.js). (2) OpenGolfAPI — automatic US fallback; small (S) effort because the live-verified /tees + /holes responses map almost 1:1 onto the existing allTees/holes shape, it needs no key, and its 1,000/day limit dwarfs the primary's 50/day; add ODbL attribution and route the wording past the Legal & Compliance reviewer before ship. (3) OSM Overpass — automatic fallback for international or remaining US misses; supplies per-hole par but no slope/rating, so persist source:'osm' and render rating/slope as 'estimated (113/par)' rather than letting the existing courses.js:292 defaults masquerade as sourced data (P9). (4) Manual entry, upgraded: link the USGA NCRDB (ncrdb.usga.org) for authoritative rating/slope lookup and lean on the already-queued scorecard-photo-scan feature (task #26) with confirm-before-save. Explicitly reject automated scraping of course websites and NCRDB (unreliable, ToS/legal RED) and reject paid aggregators (golfapi.io, iGolf, Golfbert, Zyla) under P4 zero-budget. Hedge OpenGolfAPI's young-project risk by vendoring its 2.2 MB ODbL dump as an offline snapshot. Sources: https://opengolfapi.org/ · https://github.com/opengolfapi · https://golfcourseapi.com/ · https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Tag:golf%3Dhole · https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Key:par · https://taginfo.openstreetmap.org/api/4/key/stats?key=par · https://ncrdb.usga.org/ · https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/handicapping/world-handicap-system/GPA-Program-Overview.html · https://www.sportsfirst.net/post/what-is-the-ghin-api-the-complete-guide-for-u-s-golf-app-developers · https://www.golfapi.io/ · https://igolf.com/solutions/golf-course-data/ · https://golfbert.com/

findings.area: Competitor UX research: logging a round at a course missing from the app's database (18Birdies, TheGrint, Golf GameBook, Hello Birdie, Shot Scope + Golf Pad as non-obvious benchmark)
findings.summary: Finding: NONE of the five leading apps lets a golfer log a round at an unknown course in the moment. Every one is some flavor of request-and-wait, which is the gap Parbaughs can beat outright. Per app — (1) 18Birdies: email support@18birdies.com with a photo of the scorecard and layout; staff adds it; no in-app self-service custom course and no stated SLA ("as fast as possible"). Its fastest logging path otherwise is 'Enter a Past Round' (Course + Tee + Round Type + total Score + Date, ~5 fields), but the course must already exist in the DB. (2) TheGrint: most layered but still review-gated. Web-only 'Can't find a Course?' / Course Update Tool lets users submit new/edited course info + scorecard, which TheGrint staff verify and approve; or email Courses@thegrint.com with a scorecard photo. Separately, its Scorecard Picture Service (Pro tier) transcribes a photographed paper scorecard into the app typically within ~5 minutes during staffed windows — fast, but only for courses already in the DB; minimal data = username on card + course/date/tee/holes + photo. (3) Golf GameBook: contact form / support@golfgamebook.com; they ask for the heaviest payload of the five (full scorecard incl. stroke indexes, Course Rating and Slope for all tees and genders, plus layout image for GPS) and claim they'll add it "right away" (no published SLA). Scoring works with GPS disabled, so once the course exists, scorecard-only entry is fine. (4) Hello Birdie: pure request-and-wait; email support_en@hello-birdie.com; updates land in 48-72 hours; no custom-course creation at all, and as a GPS-caddie-first app an unmapped course is essentially unusable. (5) Shot Scope: best-in-class REQUEST flow — courses visibly badged 'Not Mapped' in search, an in-app 'Request Course' button opens a form, and they commit to a ~48-hour mapping SLA, after which the user re-syncs; still a wait, because their hardware needs mapped polygons. Non-obvious benchmark: Golf Pad is the only one with true crowdsource self-service — its scout.golfpadgps.com tool lets the USER map the course in 10-20 minutes, auto-submitted for staff review, explicitly framed as the faster option vs. several-days support requests. Minimal-data floor across the industry for a 'usable' round: course identity + tee + date + per-hole or total score; par-per-hole is the only course datum actually required to render a meaningful to-par scorecard. Pattern taxonomy observed: manual-minimal entry (18Birdies past-round), photo transcription (TheGrint SPS), request-and-wait with SLA (Shot Scope 48h, Hello Birdie 48-72h), request-and-wait without SLA (18Birdies, GameBook), crowdsource-with-review (Golf Pad, TheGrint web tool). Nobody offers GPS-detect-and-stub or instant inline creation.

options.name: Request-and-wait (industry default — clone of 18Birdies/GameBook/Hello Birdie/Shot Scope)
options.how: User taps 'Course missing?' which files a task-queue/founder request (optionally with scorecard photo); Founder/agent adds the course to Firestore via GolfCourseAPI lookup or manual entry; user logs the round after it lands. Shot Scope's version is the best executed: visible 'Not Mapped' state, in-app form, published 48h SLA.
options.pros: Zero new UI surface; preserves the 'GolfCourseAPI only, zero guessing' rule perfectly; data is verified before any round references it.
options.cons: The round memory dies waiting. For a 20-friend group fresh off a golf trip, a 48-hour wall means the round gets logged in a group text instead of Parbaughs. Every competitor already does this; it beats no one.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: None — course data always verified before use.

options.name: Chart-it-yourself inline stub: play now, enrich later (RECOMMENDED CORE)
options.how: Course search (GolfCourseAPI first) misses -> 'Chart this course' inline sheet: name, town, 9/18 toggle, par-per-hole quick keypad (or total-par shortcut). ~60 seconds, round logs immediately against the new Firestore course doc flagged memberCharted:true with creator attribution. Member-charted courses render an 'Unverified — charted by {member}' badge; excluded from handicap-grade math until verified; agent later backfills/reconciles from GolfCourseAPI and Commissioner can promote to verified. Caddy announces it in editorial voice: 'First ascent — {member} charted {course}. Pars on file; rating pending.'
options.pros: Beats all five competitors outright (none allow in-the-moment logging); fits a high-trust 20-friend group where crowdsource review is just 'your buddies will roast you if the par is wrong'; minimal-data floor matches industry (par-per-hole is all a usable scorecard needs); creator-credit + Caddy 'first ascent' line turns a database gap into a community moment.
options.cons: Member-entered par data can be wrong; needs visible unverified labeling, dedupe-on-name guard, and a reconciliation path to avoid forked course docs when GolfCourseAPI later gains the course.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: Medium, mitigated: P9-compliant because data is member-attested and labeled as such (never silently fabricated); 'zero guessing' rule is honored by provenance flagging, not by blocking the round.

options.name: Scorecard photo scan auto-create (TheGrint SPS, but automated — pairs with task #26)
options.how: In the chart-it-yourself sheet, offer 'Scan the scorecard instead': photo -> vision extraction (Claude API or OSS Tesseract.js) -> prefilled hole-by-hole pars/SI/tees the member confirms before save. Also attachable AFTER a stub round as enrichment, so the photo path never gates logging. TheGrint proves photo-to-data is a beloved pattern (~5-min staff transcription, Pro-tier perk); Parbaughs automates the human out of it.
options.pros: Richest data capture (pars, stroke index, multiple tees) for ~30 seconds of user effort; upgrades a stub course to near-verified; differentiating 'wow' feature no competitor self-serves; already queued as task #26 so design effort compounds.
options.cons: OCR/vision on handwritten or glare-heavy scorecards fails often enough that it must be confirm-before-save, never auto-trusted; Claude vision is a per-call cost (Tesseract is free but markedly worse on scorecard tables); slower to ship than option 2 alone.
options.cost: ~$0.01-0.03 per scan via Claude vision, or $0 with Tesseract.js
options.dataIntegrityRisk: Low-medium: extraction errors are caught by the mandatory member-confirm step; provenance recorded as scan+confirmed.

options.name: GPS-detect + full crowdsource mapping (Golf Pad scout model)
options.how: Detect the member is standing on an unmapped course via geolocation, prompt them to drop tee/green pins per hole in a web mapping tool (Golf Pad's scout takes 10-20 min), submit for Commissioner review.
options.pros: Only model in the wild where users fully self-serve new courses; produces GPS-grade data; gold standard for a rangefinder app.
options.cons: Parbaughs is scorecard-first, not a GPS rangefinder — 10-20 minutes of pin-dropping is wildly over-spec for 'log Saturday's round'; large build (map tooling, satellite tiles, review UI) for 20 users; violates KISS/YAGNI at this scale.
options.dataIntegrityRisk: Low for positions, but huge effort-to-value mismatch.

findings.recommendation: Adopt Option 2 (chart-it-yourself inline stub) as the core flow, with Option 3 (scorecard photo scan, task #26) layered on as the enrichment accelerator — never as the gate. The research verdict is unambiguous: all five leading apps make the golfer wait (email-and-pray at 18Birdies/GameBook, 48-72h SLAs at Shot Scope/Hello Birdie, web-tool-plus-approval at TheGrint), so 'log the round in the parking lot in under a minute' is an instant category win, and it is exactly the right shape for a 20-person friend group where trust is high and a wrong par gets corrected by group-chat ridicule within the hour. The minimal-data floor should be: course name + town + 9/18 + par per hole (total-par shortcut allowed), then date + scores — everything else (tees, slope, rating, GPS) is enrichment. Honor the 'zero guessing — GolfCourseAPI only' rule through provenance, not blocking: memberCharted:true docs carry a visible 'Charted by {member} — unverified' badge, are excluded from handicap-grade calculations until reconciled against GolfCourseAPI or Commissioner-verified, and are dedupe-guarded at creation. Give the moment to the Caddy: a first-ascent editorial line ('New ground: {member} charted {course} for the books. Pars on record; the committee will see about the rating.') turns the database miss into a Parbaughs story beat — which is the part no competitor can copy with a support inbox.
